stint_ids: Grantham, Alexander William George Herder___Hong Kong___1948
persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: there are 6 Grantham officials and 6 Grantham biographies in the corpus, so one could imagine a son or namesake holding the Hong Kong governorship while the biography subject served elsewhere; and the G.C.M.G. shown in the staff list from 1952 is not in the bio's honour list, which could hint at a different, more senior man. This fails. (1) The staff-list name is the full four-part 'Alexander William George Herder Grantham' -- an extremely distinctive forename string identical to the bio's, leaving no room for an initials-only namesake. (2) The bio's final clause 'gov. and c.-in-c., H.K., 1947' matches the stint's colony, its title 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief', and its start (listed from the 1948 edition, i.e. the first edition after a 1947 appointment). (3) The shared K.C.M.G. (1945) appears in both bio and the 1948-1951 staff rows, and the bio's whole career (cadet H.K. 1922 onward) is Hong Kong-anchored. The missing G.C.M.G. in the bio simply reflects that the bio text was last revised in 1949, before the elevation -- the 1949 version's honour list ends at K.C.M.G. (1945), exactly what the staff list shows through 1951. A son born early enough to govern in 1948 would conflict with the bio subject's own documented tenure of the same post.

evidence_cited.stint_id: Grantham, Alexander William George Herder___Hong Kong___1948
Grantham, Alexander William George Herder___Hong Kong___1948
Grantham, Alexander William George Herder___Hong Kong___1948

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second Alexander William George Herder Grantham (e.g. a separate biography with a different birth year claiming the 1947 Hong Kong governorship), or an external record showing the biography subject left Hong Kong before 1957 while the staff listing continued for someone else.

notes: Five agreeing edition-years (1953-1957) with a colony-agreeing position match passed the strict edition co-occurrence check. The G.C.M.G. appearing in staff rows from 1952 postdates the last bio revision (1949 version) and is an honour progression, not a conflict.
